titleStmt.title: Letter from Caitlín Ní Shéaghda to Mabel FitzGerald, 30 May 1916.
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Caitlín Ní Shéaghda

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Caitlín Ní Shéaghda to Mabel FitzGerald (née Washington McConnell) (1884-1958), wife of politician and intellectual Desmond FitzGerald (1888-1947). The writer sympathises with Mrs FitzGerald on the severity of her husband's sentence. She enquires as to how the prisoners are treated and she asks for news of Blythe (Ernest Blythe, 1889-1975). She has heard that Mrs.O'Rahilly, widow of The O'Rahilly (1875-1916) who was killed in the fighting on 29 April 1916, gave birth to a son recently and wants confirmation.Mabel was a former governess in London. She later vigorously canvassed her former acquaintances in London's liberal circles for support for Francis Sheehy Skeffington during his hunger strike in 1915. Both Mabel and Desmond would serve in the GPO garrison during Easter Week.

correspAction.placeName: Bridge Street, Dingle, County Kerry, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Bridge Street, Dingle May 30 th 1916 Dear Mrs.Fitzgerald Please excuse me
for not writing sooner. I was very sorry for the long
sentence Desmond got but
things could be worse for you
though they are bad enough.
How must you feel at all?
You poor creature you must
feel terrible and ye were so
devoted to each other. But what of poor Desmonds
feelings they must be terrible
Can you hear from him?
or are they well treated?
What became of Blythe?
Has Mrs.O'Rahilly a
young son born to her
lately? I heard so at
all events. How are the children?
Well I hope. Have you
any notion of moving
from Bray now? Hoping to hear from
you soon again. Yours Very Sincerely CaitlÃ­n NÃ­ ShÃ©aghda
